content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN.

Consider an array A=[1,2,…,N]A = [1,2, \ldots, N]A=[1,2,…,N]. An operation on array AAA is defined as:


Erase all elements of the array AAA;
For each erased element XXX, insert all **positive** divisors of XXX into AAA.
Find the sum of all elements of AAA after KKK operations. Since this sum can be huge, print it modulo 109+710^9 +7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two space-separated integers NNN and KKK, as mentioned in the statement.


Output Format
For each test case, output the sum of all elements of AAA after KKK operations. Since this sum can be huge, print it modulo 109+710^9 +7109+7.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the sum of all elements of the array after K operations, modulo 1000000007.

solution.approach:
Let f_t(n) be the sum of all numbers that appear after t operations when we start from a single element n. The recurrence is

    f_0(n) = n
    f_{t}(n) = \sum_{d|n} f_{t-1}(d)

The operation "sum over divisors" is the Dirichlet convolution with the constant‑1 function. Hence f_t = 1^{*t} * id, where id(n)=n. Both 1 and id are multiplicative, therefore f_t is multiplicative. For a prime power n = p^e we obtain a closed form by applying the prefix‑sum operator t times:

    f_t(p^e) = \sum_{i=0}^{e} C(e-i + t - 1, t - 1) * p^i   (mod M)

where C is the binomial coefficient and M = 1e9+7. The exponent e is at most log₂ N (≤20), so the binomial can be computed on the fly even when K is as large as 10^18:

    C(K + d - 1, d) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} (K + j) / j   (mod M)

Only d ≤ e ≤ 20 terms are needed.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Pre‑compute the smallest prime factor (SPF) for every integer up to max N of all test cases (sieve O(maxN log log maxN)).
2. For each i from 1 to N factorise i using SPF. For each prime power p^e compute the term above, multiply the contributions of all prime powers – this gives f_K(i).
3. Accumulate the sum of f_K(i) for i = 1..N.
The total work over all test cases is O( Σ N * log N ) which fits the limits ( Σ N ≤ 10^6 ).
solution.timeComplexity: O( Σ N * log N ) – factorisation of each number using SPF, each factor contributes at most 20 operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(maxN) for the SPF array and O(maxExponent) for modular inverses (negligible).
